axioms.name: apparatus_component
axioms.cite: rule-stated language requires an apparatus of types and tokens

axioms.name: apparatus_component
axioms.cite: rule-stated language requires an apparatus of types and tokens

axioms.name: samsrsta
axioms.cite: the class is samsrsta, connected — riding in on the individual

rejected_readings.label: jati_monopoly
rejected_readings.rendering: The form-universal (jāti) is the sole and sufficient basis for grammatical reference, with the individual form (vyakti) playing no essential role.
rejected_readings.why: Directly contradicts the commentary's assertion that 'rule-stated language requires an apparatus of types and tokens' — an irreducible coordination. Denying vyakti's role violates the apparatus structure both reported positions affirm.

rejected_readings.label: conflated_positions
rejected_readings.rendering: The individual form (vyakti) and the class (jāti) play interchangeable roles in grammatical reference, or are functionally equivalent.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's force rests on distinguishing two positions: position 1 assigns vyakti and jāti different roles (vyakti as name, jāti as connected operator), while position 2 assigns them mediated roles (vyakti as named-through-jāti). Conflating them erases the reported distinction, which the commentary emphasizes as the 'akrti/vyakti controversy'.

notes: The verse reports the classical akṛti/vyakti debate within Pāninian exegesis: whether reference is to vyakti-as-name (v.68) or vyakti-as-named-through-jāti (v.69). Bhartṛhari's stance is to accept both as confirming a structural requirement: language needs an apparatus of types and tokens in coordination. The contract captures this shared structural point rather than choosing between the positions.
